title: test/system: speed up basic_{setup,teardown}()
body:
While these are not really slow they still take about 100-250ms if I time this locally. Given they are run for every test this adds up quickly. Looking at CI logs I can see the timings for skipped tests are all in 600ms range. So I think it is safe to assume that these function need to get faster.

We have over 670 test cases currently so we talk about over 400s spend in these functions in CI. I think this allows big gains

Now overall this is a tricky trade of, while all tests should cleanup after themselves there is no guarantee for that as such errors can be leaked into other tests making debugging much harder. To work at least a bit against this teardown checks if the test was successful and only skips the podman commnds bases on that. Without it a single flake could cause all following test to fail.


see commits for more details

comments.body:
>most tests should do the proper cleanup already hopefully.

hahahahahahahaha, what wonderful optimism. But I am really grateful that you're taking on this work. It's something I've started on and given up because it's so difficult.

Tests should clean up after themselves: absolutely. This PR is a good way to find tests that don't.

However, we still need a fallback cleanup for tests that fail: the nuke-stray-images thing is still needed in helpers.bash, not suite.bash, because we need the _option_ of running it after failed tests. Perhaps in teardown, if there's a way to check exit status of a test? Or perhaps in file_teardown for tests that do build/load/image manipulation?

> Perhaps in teardown, if there's a way to check exit status of a test?

Well yeah that is what I added here `$BATS_TEST_COMPLETED`
https://bats-core.readthedocs.io/en/stable/faq.html#how-can-i-check-if-a-test-failed-succeeded-during-teardown
looks like it is green now, I have to cleanup up commits first before it is good for review but this looks promising I think

Ok I think this should look much better now. Of course this is a risk thing. If a test forgets to do cleanup it might cause a failure much later or never when it is one of the later tests.

Should I add a teardown_suite that checks that there no leaks at the end of all tests? Also for local testing via hack/bats I turned on the leak check on so local development will tell the users if the tests is written incorrect at least.

| type | distro              | user     | DB     |    local |   remote | container |
| ---- | --------            | ----     | --     |    ----: |   -----: | --------: |
| sys  | rawhide             | root     |        |    31:41 |   !20:15 |
| sys  | rawhide             | rootless |        |    31:03 |          |
| sys  | fedora-40-aarch64   | root     |        |    25:09 |   !17:02 |
| sys  | fedora-40           | root     |        |    29:36 |   !18:49 |
| sys  | fedora-40           | rootless |        |    30:52 |   !19:21 |
| sys  | fedora-39           | root     | boltdb |    32:45 |   !20:03 |
| sys  | fedora-39           | rootless | boltdb |    32:11 |          |
| sys  | debian-13           | root     |        |    31:43 |   !21:57 |
| sys  | debian-13           | rootless |        |    29:24 |          |

I think this proves a measurable speed up. One remote test is still failing but I don't see this impacting the timing.
Even after my 3 previous test speedup PR's the local runs were still close or even over 40 mins, here we are around 30 mins. Gaining 10 mins is a lot!

Timings with the leak check activated (+ ~5mins more), thus I think it is fair to push for a no leak check. It will make maintenance/reviews harder as catching these leaks is hard but we always have to option to just turn it on vie the env var.
And I guess we could turn on the env only in the nightly runs where timing is not as important. 

| type | distro              | user     | DB     |    local |   remote | container |
| ---- | --------            | ----     | --     |    ----: |   -----: | --------: |
| sys  | rawhide             | root     |        |    32:08 |    22:06 |
| sys  | rawhide             | rootless |        |    33:49 |          |
| sys  | fedora-40-aarch64   | root     |        |    28:47 |    19:39 |
| sys  | fedora-40           | root     |        |    32:13 |    21:37 |
| sys  | fedora-40           | rootless |        |    33:21 |    21:30 |
| sys  | fedora-39           | root     | boltdb |    35:33 |    24:15 |
| sys  | fedora-39           | rootless | boltdb |    35:52 |          |
| sys  | debian-13           | root     |        |    37:46 |    24:28 |
| sys  | debian-13           | rootless |        |    37:39 |          |

> This is still set to Draft. What is your expectation, timing-wise, for this to merge? Given the lack of personnel this week I have a medium-high preference (7 out of 10) for waiting until next week. I'm willing to listen to counterarguments.

I left it as draft until I have your LGTM. I agree given we both are out for the second half of the week and this has the risk to cause regressions, especially when other in flight PRs are merged without proper test cleanup.

So I keep it as draft until next Monday then I rebase (hopefully don't have to deal with new broken tests) and we can merge.
Still a draft or should we merge?
@rhatdan we will merge next week. Paul and I are OOTO for the rest of this week; it might be unwise to introduce high-risk changes.
Ok I just did a new leak test run to make sure no new leaks were added over the last week. Now I pushed it again without it so this is ready.
